of how it works. Not the equations — we can leave those to the specialists — but the cast of characters:
. With those words in place, the rest of the course makes sense.

By the end of this chapter you should be able to explain, in plain language and over coffee, what happens when somebody says “we trained a model on a billion images and now we are using it to generate logos”.
A toy starting point: learning from examples

children.label: A toy starting point: learning from examples

children.value: Imagine you want to teach a computer to tell
in photographs. You have two strategies:
“If the ears are pointy and the snout is narrow…” — this is the
AI of the mid-20th century. It works for small problems and breaks for anything visual at the level of the real world.

children.alt: A simple diagram showing data flowing into a model with parameters, producing a prediction, with a loss feedback loop into the parameters.

children.value: measures how wrong the prediction is and is used to update the parameters.

The diagram above is essentially the whole field. The specifics — what the data looks like, what shape the model takes, how the loss is measured — change wildly between applications. But the loop is always the same.
. Every AI model you will use in this course was trained on a dataset:
collections), licensed stock libraries, scraped web galleries.
— books, Wikipedia, Common Crawl scrapes of the open web, scientific papers, code repositories.
— music libraries, podcasts, speech corpora, YouTube transcripts.
— public video platforms, licensed footage libraries, motion-capture archives.
Pick a tool you have used and try to find a public statement about
it was trained on. How precise is the answer?
of training data shape the model in deep ways. We will return to this in chapter
. In modern AI, this function is a

— a layered chain of multiplications, additions, and non-linear “squashing” operations. The parameters are the numbers (often billions of them) that determine exactly which input produces which output.
You do not need to know the inner workings to use a model, just as you do not need to know how a violin is made to play one. But two facts matter:
This means we can compute, for each parameter,
which direction would make the prediction slightly better
. That is what makes training possible.
Modern models have between a few hundred million and a few trillion parameters. The model file alone can be tens of gigabytes.
taking a batch of examples from the dataset,
making predictions with the current parameters,
nudging the parameters in the direction that reduces the loss.

for a large model. It typically takes days or weeks on hundreds of high-end GPUs and consumes enormous amounts of electricity. Strubell et al. estimated the carbon cost of training a large NLP model already in 2019; the numbers have grown since
The result of training is a
: the network plus its specific set of parameter values.

a trained model. You feed it an input (a prompt, an image, an audio clip), and it produces an output. Inference is far cheaper than training — a few cents instead of a few million dollars — but at the scale of hundreds of millions of users it still adds up.
When you type into ChatGPT, you are
on a previously trained model. The model’s parameters do not change.
Most of the AI you use day to day is doing
, not training. The model already exists; you are just asking it questions.
We want a model that does well on
examples it has never seen. This is
— the model has memorised the training examples and does poorly on anything else.

Generalisation is the reason a face-recognition model can recognise a face it never saw during training. It is also the reason a language model can write a paragraph about a topic that did not exist in its training set. (And, as we will see, the reason such a paragraph can be subtly wrong.)

. If a dataset over-represents English-speaking, Western, web-published, well-photographed material, the model will be best at exactly that material. This is not a bug; it is a property of how machine learning works
. We will return to bias as an ethical and practical question in chapter
, but it is also relevant to everyday creative use.
A very short tour of neural networks

children.label: A very short tour of neural networks

children.value: . Each layer takes a list of numbers, multiplies them by another list of numbers (the parameters), adds, and passes the result through a simple non-linear function. Stacked many times, this becomes flexible enough to model very complex patterns
A few common families you will hear about:
— used for images for most of the 2010s.
— used for sequences (text, audio) until around 2018.
. Powers most chat models, image models, and the new generation of audio and video models.
layered on top of a neural network, particularly successful for images
The specific architecture matters for performance, but the cast of characters above stays the same.
Most state-of-the-art creative AI tools today are
: very large models trained once, then
to many tasks. Adaptation can take several forms:
— putting the right text in front of the model at inference time. No retraining needed.

— taking a foundation model and continuing to train it briefly on a smaller, more focused dataset. Costs vary from a few dollars (LoRA on a personal GPU) to millions (full fine-tunes of large models).
— reinforcement learning from human or AI feedback. Used to align chat models to be helpful and polite.
— training a smaller “student” model to mimic a larger “teacher”.

Part 2 — A 10-line training loop (60 min, optional code track)

children.label: Part 2 — A 10-line training loop (60 min, optional code track)

children.value: This is for students who want to feel a training loop in their fingers. It is
We will train a tiny model to fit a curve. Open a notebook and run:

import numpy as np

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
x = rng.uniform(-1, 1, size=200)
y = 2 * x + 0.5 + rng.normal(0, 0.1, size=200)

# parameters
w, b = 0.0, 0.0
lr = 0.05

for step in range(200):
    pred = w * x + b
    loss = ((pred - y) ** 2).mean()
    grad_w = ((pred - y) * x).mean() * 2
    grad_b = (pred - y).mean() * 2
    w -= lr * grad_w
    b -= lr * grad_b

print(f"w = {w:.3f}, b = {b:.3f}, loss = {loss:.4f}")

children.html: w≈2w \approx 2w≈2
b≈0.5b \approx 0.5b≈0.5

children.value: . That is a model with two parameters, trained from scratch, in 12 lines. Every “AI” you will use this semester is the same loop scaled up by a factor of ten billion.
List three categories of work in your discipline where AI models are
useful, and three where you suspect they are not. What is the difference?
The training data of large models is mostly English, mostly Western, and mostly from the open web. How might that show up in a model’s outputs for your discipline or your native language?
Strubell et al. estimated training a large NLP model could emit as much CO₂ as five cars over their lifetimes
. How does that change (or not) how you feel about using these tools?
